coredata.dc:title: Capillary electrophoretic study of amine/carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qin
Paau, Man Chin
Zhang, Yan
Chan, Wan
Gong, Xiaojuan
Zhang, Lei
Choi, Martin M.F.
coredata.dc:description: Capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE) coupled with UV absorption and laser-induced fluorescence detections has been applied to study the complexity of carbon nanodots (C-dots) products synthesized with microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA). The effects of pH and concentration of run buffer on the CZE separation of C-dots are studied in detail. The optimal acetate run buffer (30mM, pH 3.6) is subsequently employed to investigate the effect of reaction time and mole ratio of amine (NH2) to carboxylic acid (COOH) moieties of the precursors on the C-dots species present in C-dots products. Our results confirm that the synthesis of C-dots could be improved by lengthening the microwave irradiation time and optimizing the initial mole ratio of NH2/COOH in the precursors. Negatively charged C-dots are obtained only when the amount of CA exceeds that of EDA, i.e., the mole ratio of NH2/COOH is 0.25–0.80. By contrast, when the quantity (mole) of NH2 in EDA is equal to or larger than that of COOH in CA, only positively charged and neutral C-dots species are formed, inferring that the C-dots species are predominantly covered by the surface-attached ammonium and amido moieties. This work highlights the merit of CZE to identify the composition of an as-prepared C-dots product which is pretty much dependent on the mole ratio of NH2/COOH. It is anticipated that our CZE methodology will open a new avenue in optimizing the synthetic conditions for producing specific C-dots of desired composition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amine and carboxylic acid functionality
Carbon dots
Synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Capillary zone electrophoretic separation of C-dots
Effect of capillary, temperature, applied voltage, and sample injection
Effect of reaction time on C-dots product
Effect of ratio of amine to carboxylic acid groups on C-dots product

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the as-prepared C-dots. The inset photograph shows the SAED pattern of C-dots. (B) The particle size distribution of C-dots is obtained by counting randomly 100 particles from various spots of the sample during TEM imaging.

Electropherograms of C-dots obtained with run buffer of 30mM NaAc–HAc at different pH: (a) 3.4, (b) 3.6, (c) 3.8, (d) 4.0, (e) 4.5, (f) 5.0, and (g) 6.0. Electropherograms are offset for clarity and ease of comparison. The inset displays the plots of (1) μ ep of Peak 1 (positively charged C-dots), (2) μ eof of Peak 2 (neutral C-dots) and (3) μ ep of Peak 3 (negatively charged C-dots) against pH.

Electropherograms of C-dots obtained at different NaAc–HAc (pH 3.6) run buffer concentrations: (a) 10, (b) 20, (c) 30, (d) 40, and (e) 50mM. Electropherograms are offset for clarity and ease of comparison. The inset depicts the plots of (1) μ ep of Peak 1 (positively charged C-dots), (2) μ eof of Peak 2 (neutral C-dots) and (3) μ ep of Peak 3 (negatively charged C-dots) against buffer concentration.

Electropherograms of aqueous solutions of C-dots synthesized with 0.67 mole ratio of amine/carboxylic acid group at different reaction times: (a) 1.0, (b) 2.0, (c) 3.0, (d) 4.0, (e) 5.0, and (f) 6.0min. Run buffer is 30mM NaAc–HAc (pH 3.6). Electropherograms are offset for clarity and ease of comparison. The peaks labeled with asterisks are neutral C-dots. Peaks migrated before and after neutral C-dots are positively and negatively charged C-dots, respectively.

Electropherograms of aqueous solutions of C-dots synthesized with 4.0min reaction time and different mole ratios of amine/carboxylic acid group in precursors: (a) 0.00, (b) 0.25, (c) 0.50, (d) 0.67, (e) 0.80, (f) 1.00, (g) 1.33, and (h) 2.00. Run buffer is 30mM NaAc–HAc and pH 3.6. Electropherograms are offset for clarity and ease of comparison. The peaks labeled with asterisks are neutral C-dots. Peaks migrated before and after neutral C-dots are positively and negatively charged C-dots, respectively.

head.ce:title: Capillary electrophoretic study of amine/carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, 224 Waterloo Road, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Research Center of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Research Center of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Division of Environment, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Kowloon, Hong Kong SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Division of Environment, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Exchange student on visit to Hong Kong Baptist University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE) coupled with UV absorption and laser-induced fluorescence detections has been applied to study the complexity of carbon nanodots (C-dots) products synthesized with microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA). The effects of pH and concentration of run buffer on the CZE separation of C-dots are studied in detail. The optimal acetate run buffer (30mM, pH 3.6) is subsequently employed to investigate the effect of reaction time and mole ratio of amine (NH2) to carboxylic acid (COOH) moieties of the precursors on the C-dots species present in C-dots products. Our results confirm that the synthesis of C-dots could be improved by lengthening the microwave irradiation time and optimizing the initial mole ratio of NH2/COOH in the precursors. Negatively charged C-dots are obtained only when the amount of CA exceeds that of EDA, i.e., the mole ratio of NH2/COOH is 0.25–0.80. By contrast, when the quantity (mole) of NH2 in EDA is equal to or larger than that of COOH in CA, only positively charged and neutral C-dots species are formed, inferring that the C-dots species are predominantly covered by the surface-attached ammonium and amido moieties. This work highlights the merit of CZE to identify the composition of an as-prepared C-dots product which is pretty much dependent on the mole ratio of NH2/COOH. It is anticipated that our CZE methodology will open a new avenue in optimizing the synthetic conditions for producing specific C-dots of desired composition.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) are newcomers to the nanoparticles (NPs) world and they constitute a fascinating class of recently discovered nanocarbons which possess discrete, quasi-spherical, nanocrystalline, or amorphous carbon structure with diameters less than 10nm [1–6]. C-dots have drawn considerable attention as nascent quantum dots (QDs) in the fields of lasers [7], photocatalysis [8] and energy conversion [9], thanks to their typically excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior. Their tunable emission and broad excitation wavelengths, excellent biocompatibility and chemical stability also make them very attractive for bioimaging [10]. Besides, C-dots stand to have significant potential for environmental and health applications as they are possible nontoxic replacements to traditional semiconductor QDs which are usually composed of toxic metals.

Notably, the typical PL properties of C-dots play a dominant role in rendering their viability in a number of applications. Although the origins of C-dots luminescence have not yet been sorely understood and no agreements have been achieved, a widely accepted mechanism for PL is the radiative recombination of excitons at the surface energy traps [1–4]. In addition, the surface functionality on C-dots appears to be influential on their PL properties [10,11]. So far a great deal of efforts has been focused on contriving better synthetic routes to produce C-dots with desirable PL performance. Recent advancements in this area include electrochemical oxidation [12,13], laser ablation [4,14,15], hydrothermal synthesis [16–18], pyrolysis [5,6,19], and microwave-assisted heating [10,11,20–22]. Surface passivation was found to be capable of enhancing PL properties of C-dots in many studies. The most popular technique is to use nitrogen-containing compounds such as PEG1500N [4,15], PEG200N [20], diamine hydrate and diethanolamine [23]. As the composition of as-synthesized C-dots is so complex, it is vital to develop an efficient analytical separation methodology to identify the individual C-dots species present in an as-prepared C-dots product more precisely.

Polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) [24] and anion-exchange high-performance liquid chromatography (AE-HPLC) [25,26] have been applied to separate and fractionate C-dots, each with their merits and shortcomings. PAGE has virtue in identifying the relationship between the mobility and color of the fluorescent C-dots; nevertheless, it does not possess high separation efficiency. AE-HPLC using ammonium salts as eluent could achieve high-resolution separation of C-dots mixture and the preparative scale property of HPLC allows for the collection of individual fractions for further analysis. However, fraction collection is time-consuming and the workload is further exacerbated by removing the ammonium salts from the collected fractions.

Capillary electrophoresis (CE) has rapidly emerged as one of the most powerful approach for high-efficient separation of NPs [27,28]. Although C-dots derived from soot [29], graphene oxide and graphene [30] have been analyzed by CE, there is not much work on applying this technique to study the effect of synthetic conditions on the formation and property of C-dots product. Herein, we choose to apply a microwave-pyrolysis method to synthesize C-dots, as it is simple, fast and straightforward [10,11]. Although PL spectroscopy has been applied to investigate the influence of reaction conditions including reaction time and mole ratios of NH2/COOH [10,11,31] on the PL properties of the resulting C-dots products; this method can only provide the summation or average properties of a complex C-dots mixture. In order to take advantages of C-dots practically nontoxic property, one needs to fully know their composition such that their significant properties can be used widely. In this article, we report the separation of C-dots products by capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE) coupled with UV absorption and laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) detections. The optimal CZE conditions such as pH and concentration of the run buffer were investigated in detail. Our proposed CZE methodology has been successfully applied to study the reaction time-associated kinetics of C-dots formation and identify the functional groups-associated charge states (i.e., neutral, positive or negative) of each separated C-dots fractions, facilitating the understanding of how different mole ratios of NH2/COOH in precursors affect the surface-functionality of the C-dots species in a complex C-dots product. To our knowledge, this is the first report on separating positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots simultaneously, highlighting the values of modern analytical separation for tuning the synthesis of NPs.

1,2-Ethylenediamine was obtained from Acros Organics (Geel, Belgium). Anhydrous citric acid and 2,5-dihydroxbenzoic acid were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Sodium dihydrogen phosphate monohydrate was obtained from Mallinckrodt Chemical (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). Sodium hydrogen phosphate and glacial acetic acid (HAc) were from Fisher Chemicals (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA). Sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). N,N′-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and methanol were from Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand). Absolute ethanol was from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Distilled deionized (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity higher than 18MΩcm (Bedford, MA, USA). All reagents of analytical reagent grade or above were used as received without further purification.

The capillary electrophoretic separations were carried out with a Beckman-Coulter P/ACE MDQ CE system (Fullerton, CA, USA) in conjunction with a diode array detector monitoring at 250nm or a laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) detector monitoring at excitation/emission wavelengths of 488/550nm. Data collection and system operation were controlled by v. 7.0 32 Karat software (Beckman-Coulter). Separations were accomplished by a bare fused-silica capillary 50μm i.d. and 325μm o.d. (Polymicro Technologies, Phoenix, AZ, USA). The total and effective lengths of the capillary were 40.0cm and 30.0cm, respectively, with a detection window fabricated 10.0cm from the capillary outlet. New capillaries were initialized by flushing with DDI water (5min), 1.0M NaOH (15min), 0.10M NaOH (15min), DDI water (30min), and run buffer (30min). Between each analysis, the capillary was rinsed sequentially with DDI water (2.0min), 0.10M NaOH (2.0min), DDI water (2.0min), and run buffer (5.0min). Unless otherwise stated, aqueous C-dots solutions (ca. 10mg/mL) were injected hydrodynamically at 0.5psi for 3.0s. The temperature of capillary cartridge was set at 25°C and the applied voltage for electrophoretic separation was 15kV. Run buffer was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of NaAc in DDI water and the desired pH values were adjusted by dropwise addition of HAc. The pH of buffer solutions was measured by an Orion model 410A pH meter (Allometrics Inc., Baton Rouge, CA, USA). Each 245μL aqueous solution of C-dots was mixed with 5.0μL DMF (0.100M) as the neutral marker. All run buffers and C-dots samples for CZE analysis were filtered through 0.45μm, 13mm i.d. cellulose acetate syringe filters (Alltech, Deerfield, IL, USA) prior to injection.

Safety precaution. C-dots are newcomers to the nanoparticle world and the study of their toxicity is still under investigation. Although there is no solid evidence to prove that C-dots are completely non-hazardous, eyewear and gloves should be used to avoid exposure to C-dots. Waste C-dots are disposed and transported in solution according to the hazardous waste procedures for solvents.

Various optical determinations were employed to characterize the as-synthesized C-dots. As all the C-dots products synthesized under different reaction conditions possess similar spectral characteristics, C-dots product synthesized with 4.0min microwave irradiation and 0.67 mole ratio of NH2/COOH was used to perform the tests unless otherwise stated. The experimental details of synthesis, characterization and quantum yield (Φ S) determination of C-dots are deposited in Supplementary data.

Fig. S1A (Supplementary data) depicts the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the C-dots sample. It shows strong UV–vis absorption with a prominent peak at 350nm assigned to n → π* transitions of CO bonding and a shoulder band at 240nm originated from the π → π* transition of the aromatic carbons on the C-core [32]. The C-dots sample depicts an emission peak at 448nm upon excitation at 350nm, displaying strong blue emission under a hand-held UV lamp (Inset of Fig. S1A). The quantum yield of the as-synthesized C-dots is 42.8% (Fig. S1B and Supplementary data) which is in consistent with the literature values [3,10]. Fig. S1C depicts the PL spectra of C-dots and the inset shows the normalized emission spectra under various excitation wavelengths. The emission spectra are slightly shifted from 442 to 460nm as the excitation wavelength varies from 310 to 410nm, inferring that the C-dots sample possibly comprises various C-dots species. It has been suggested that the excitation dependent PL behavior of C-dots could arise from the “surface states” formed by the size effect and different functionalities of the C-dots [33,34]. Thus, we anticipate that the shift in emission wavelength of our C-dots sample is attributed to the presence of various C-dots species which is verified by our CZE analysis (vide infra). Each C-dots species probably possesses their unique spectral characteristics, depending on C-core size and surface-functionality.

TEM has traditionally been used to obtain size information about NPs. Fig. 1A displays a typical TEM image of the as-synthesized C-dots and the inset is its selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. The absence of any discernible ring suggests an amorphous nature of the as-prepared C-dots. Herein, it should take note that C-dots tend to aggregate during sample preparation for TEM analysis [25,35]. Thus, it is common to observe multiple low-contrast aggregates in the TEM image of C-dots sample. The size distribution histogram in Fig. 1B is obtained by counting randomly 100 particles from various spots of the sample during TEM imaging. Other TEM images are deposited as Fig. S2A and B in Supplementary data. The C-dots sample has a size range of 2.2–5.0nm and an average diameter of 3.4±0.2nm. As the as-synthesized C-dots sample is quite polydisperse, high-resolution analytical separation technique such as CZE is needed to analyze the complex nature of C-dots (vide infra).

IR spectroscopy is a useful technique to characterize the surface functionality of C-dots. The IR spectrum of the as-synthesized C-dots is depicted in Fig. S3A. The IR spectra of CA and EDA were also examined for comparison (Fig. S3B and C). The C-dots display a broad peak at 3200–3500cm−1 corresponding to the O–H and N–H stretch of the carboxylic acid and amine groups. Strong absorptions at 1708, 1650, 1556, 1404, and 1180cm−1 corresponding to the CO, CC stretching, NH bending vibration, amido CN, and amine CN stretching bands are observed. In addition, a shoulder peak at around 2845–2975cm−1 corresponding to the CH vibrational stretch is found for the C-dots. For CA, two characteristic broad peaks ascribed to OH and CO stretch are observed. For EDA, two characteristic peaks attributing to NH bending (1574cm−1) and CN stretching (1092cm−1) of amines are identified. The IR spectra suggest that the possible amidation reaction should have taken place between CA and EDA and the C-dots surface is covered with the amine, amide and carboxylic acid groups which are consistent with the literature [10,11]. More importantly, the IR spectra of C-dots possesses a distinctive absorption peak at 1650cm−1 which is absent from the IR spectra of CA and EDA, indicating the presence of CC unsaturated bond on the C-dots which are typical in other fluorescent C-dots [22,36]. The functional groups identified in the IR spectra of CA, EDA and C-dots are summarized in Table S1 (Supplementary data). In summary, the C-dots comprise sp 2- and sp 3-hybridized carbons core and surface-attached amine, amide and carboxylic acid moieties. These surface functionalities impart to the hydrophilicity, stability and excellent solubility of the C-dots in aqueous solution, facilitating its separation and analysis by CZE.

Mass spectroscopy (MS) is a valuable tool in identifying the mass of NPs. Fig. S4A displays the MS of the as-synthesized C-dots product. The highest abundance and largest mass ions are m/z 1645 (tag with blue asterisk) and 3154 (tag with red asterisk), respectively. Although it is difficult to deduce the exact chemical structure of the C-dots from its MS, the expanded MS spectra definitely give insights into the surface chemistry of C-dots. When the MS is expanded at the mass ranges 1150–1550, 1620–1880, and 1900–2300Da (Fig. S4B–D), it is observed that the MS peaks show a series of major m/z spacing in the mass units of 16, 18 and 45, corresponding to the loss of NH2, H2O and COOH fragments, respectively. These MS data concur with our IR spectra that the C-dots are covered with amine, amide and carboxylic acid moieties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capillary zone electrophoretic separation of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: As the as-synthesized C-dots comprise amine, amide and carboxylic acid moieties, it will readily exist as ions in aqueous solution system. CZE will be an ideal analytical separation technique to study their electrophoretic behavior. Efforts are initially directed to optimize parameters including pH, run buffer concentration, applied voltage, capillary temperature, injection time and pressure, and capillary internal diameter and length for CZE separation of C-dots. Experiments were performed with C-dots typically synthesized with 4.0min microwave irradiation time and 0.67 mole ratio of NH2/COOH in the precursors since a better yield of C-dots is obtained under these synthetic conditions. This C-dots product is found to contain three major types, i.e., positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots by CZE analysis (vide infra).

Fig. 2 depicts the effect of run buffer pH 3.4–6.0 (30mM NaAc–HAc) on the electrophoretic separation of C-dots. The electropherograms show C-dots species with a wide range of mobilities, indicating the anticipated complexity of a C-dots sample. In general, C-dots species are divided into three major types, depending on their overall charges. Peak 2 is neutral C-dots as its migration time is same as the neutral marker DMF. Since DMF does not have appreciable absorption at 250nm, it will not mask the detection of neutral C-dots. Peaks migrated before and after neutral C-dots are positively and negatively charged C-dots species, respectively, since the CZE system is run under cathodic flow. The migration times for all the C-dots species decrease with the increase in pH from 3.4 to 6.0. When pH increases, the neutral (Peak 2) and positively charged (Peak 1) C-dots migrate closer with each other and finally they are co-migrated at pH 4.5–6.0. These changes are attributed to the variation of electroosmotive flow (μ eof) and electrophoretic mobility (μ ep) of C-dots with pH. μ eof and μ ep can be calculated from μ eof = lL/Vt o and μ ep =(lL/V)[(1/t)−(1/t o)], where l is the distance between the inlet and the detector, L is the total length of the capillary, V is the applied voltage, and t o and t are the migration times of the neutral and charged C-dots, respectively [37]. The inset of Fig. 2 depicts the plots of μ eof or μ ep against pH. The μ eof increases with the increase in pH (curve 2 in inset), attributing to the generation of a larger number of negative charges on the capillary wall with a concomitant effect on increasing the zeta potential. The μ ep of the positively charged C-dots (Peak 1, curve 1 in inset) decreases with the increase in pH and reaches very low μ ep at pH≥4.5. Since the C-dots species are surface-attached with amine and carboxylic acid moieties, the net charge of each C-dots species is determined by the charges of these moieties in terms of NH3 + and COO− at a specific pH. It is well known that the amine group is mostly present as NH3 + form at pH<10 while the carboxylic acid group exists as COO− when pH>4.5. Thus, C-dots appear to be positively, neutral or negatively charged if the quantity of NH3 + exceeds, equal to or less than that of COO−, respectively. As such, it can be visualized that Peak 1 initially presents as positively charged C-dots at pH 3.4. But it will be less positive when the pH increases as its surface-attached COOH moiety starts to convert to COO−. When pH≥4.5, the μ ep of Peak 1 equals to zero because the net charge of this C-dots species is zero (curve 1 in inset). As a result, it is co-migrated with Peak 2, i.e., EOF. For Peak 2, it is a neutral C-dots species as its net charge is zero. Its peak area increases with pH since more positively charged C-dots species are converted to the neutral form when pH increases. The μ ep of Peak 3 increases with pH (curve 3 in inset) as it turns to be more negative, accompanying with the dissociation of COOH to COO−. In essence, the migration times of all the peaks decrease with the increase in pH as the changes in μ eof are always larger than that of μ ep, so that the bulk flow (EOF) can sweep all the C-dots species toward the cathode.

In conclusion, the separation of C-dots is inefficient at higher pH (4.5–6.0) for positively (Peak 1) and neutral C-dots species (Peak 2). They tend to cluster together and are co-migrated. Fortunately, at lower pH (3.6–3.8) the resolution for C-dots is substantially improved. All the positively charged C-dots migrated before Peak 1 are mostly separated. Peaks 1 and 2 are partially separated at pH 4.0 and completely resolved at pH 3.6–3.8. However, Peak 1 is slightly broadened when pH decreases to 3.4. As such, 3.6 is chosen as the optimal pH for this work as it provides the best separation and reasonable migration time for most C-dots species in the sample.

In addition, the C-dots product was analyzed under alkaline conditions using phosphate run buffers. The electrophoretic separation of C-dots over the pH range 7.0–12.0 (Fig. S5) is unsatisfactory. All electropherograms show a series of unresolved peaks comprising positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots species superimposed on a broad solute band. There are more negatively charged C-dots species when pH is higher than 11.0 since all the surface-attached ammonium and carboxylic acid groups are depronated; unfortunately they all co-migrate together as a broad band. As a result, alkaline run buffer is not suitable for CZE analysis of our C-dots samples.

The effect of run buffer concentration on capillary electrophoretic behavior was also investigated. Fig. 3A displays the CZE separation of C-dots at buffer concentration 10–50mM (pH 3.6). Fig. 3B depicts the plots of μ eof or μ ep against buffer concentration. Again, Peaks 1, 2 and 3 are assigned to positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots species, respectively. The μ ep of Peak 1 increases slightly with buffer concentration until it reaches a plateau at 40mM buffer. The μ eof (Peak 2) decreases with the increase in buffer concentration, attributing to the decrease in the thickness of double layer on the capillary wall and hence lower zeta potential. The μ ep of Peak 3 decreases slightly with buffer concentration until it reaches 20mM. The electropherograms show that better separation can be achieved with the increase in buffer concentration at 20–30mM. At 10mM, Peaks 1 and 2 are partially separated. At 40–50mM, the migration time of Peak 3 is long (>35min). As such, 20–30mM NaAc–HAc is chosen as the optimal buffer concentration for the separation of our as-synthesized C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of capillary, temperature, applied voltage, and sample injection
ce:section.ce:para: Besides run buffer pH and concentration, the effects of capillary internal diameter (i.d.), capillary length, applied voltage, capillary temperature, and sample injection pressure and time on CZE separation of the C-dots sample were also studied in detail. The capillary with 50μm i.d. produces shorter migration times and better separation than that of 75μm i.d. under the same applied voltage of 15kV. Peak 3 is not observed for the first 45min but is detected when the applied voltage is increased to 30kV. Unfortunately, this high applied voltage causes peak broadening of the later migrated solute due to the Joule heating effect (Fig. S6). When the capillary is short (30cm), the solutes tend to cluster together. On the other hand, if the capillary is long (60cm), the migration time for Peak 3 is too long (>60min). As such, the optimal length of the capillary is 40cm (Fig. S7). Fig. S8 depicts the effect of applied voltage on the CZE separation of C-dots sample. When the voltage is low (10kV), the migration time of Peak 3 is long (45min). Although higher applied voltage (20kV) reduces the migration times of most C-dots species, some of the C-dots species tend to cluster together. As a result, 15kV was chosen as the optimal applied voltage (Fig. S8). No significant effect on separation is observed for temperatures 15.0–35.0°C (Fig. S9). The peak areas of C-dots species increase with the increase in sample injection pressure (Fig. S10) or time (Fig. S11). The separations of the C-dots species are more or less the same. In summary, the optimal CZE conditions for separation of our as-synthesized C-dots product are: 40.0cm capillary with 50μm i.d. and 325μm o.d, applied voltage 15kV, capillary temperature 25.0°C, and sample injection pressure 0.5psi and time 3.0s.

Herein, it is necessary to point out that the CZE method is technically challenging in the analysis of C-dots. The baseline continuously drifts downward and the migration time of the negatively charged C-dots shifts significantly for the first 5–6h run time; thus degrades the separation. In order to circumvent this situation, the CE instrument is normally warmed up for 12h prior to sample analysis. Fig. S12 depicts the five continuous injections of C-dots sample. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of migration time and peak area for Peaks 1–3 are summarized in Table S2. All RSDs are below 2.94%, demonstrating the high repeatability of our CZE method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time on C-dots product
ce:section.ce:para: It is anticipated that the reaction time of CA and EDA by microwave irradiation will affect the formation of C-dots as well as its composition. A series of C-dots products were synthesized by microwave heating the CA and EDA mixture (0.67 mole ratio of NH2/COOH) at various reaction times (1.0–6.0min). After the reaction, the crude C-dots products were cleansed by dialysis and lyophilized until further use (for details see Supplementary data). Fig. S13 displays the photographic images of the aqueous C-dots solutions synthesized with 1.0–6.0min. The top and bottom images are under day light and UV irradiation, respectively. At 1.0min, the solution is transparent and no blue emission is observed (Fig. S13a). When the reaction time is longer than 1.0min, the solutions turn to brownish and display strong blue emissions under UV (Fig. S13b–f). The solutions are more brownish and show stronger emission as the reaction time lengthens. The blue emission increases at 2.0–3.0min (Fig. S13b and c) and decreases at 4.0–6.0min (Fig. S13d–f). The decrease in emission is attributing to the fact that these solutions contain very high concentrations of C-dots, resulting in self-quenching. After three folds dilution with water, strong blue emissions are recovered (far right images of Fig. S13). These results confirm that longer reaction time could produce larger amounts of C-dots which are in complete agreement with our CZE analysis (vide infra).

The dry C-dots products synthesized with various reaction times were dissolved in 5.0mL water to obtain a series of C-dots aqueous solutions which are then subjected to CZE analysis under the optimal run buffer (30mM NaAc–HAc, pH 3.6). Fig. 4 displays the electropherograms of C-dots products synthesized with different reaction times. Peaks labeled with asterisks are neutral C-dots. Peaks migrated before and after neutral C-dots are positively and negatively charged C-dots species. It is observed that no C-dots are formed at 1.0min (Fig. 4a). The reason is that water in the reaction mixture has to be completely evaporated before the pyrolysis of CA and EDA could initiate to generate C-dots [31]. Regrettably, 1.0min is not long enough for water evaporation and therefore no C-dots are formed. When the reaction time lengthens from 1.0 to 6.0min, larger and numerous peaks are found in the electropherograms (Fig. 4b–f). All C-dots species increase proportionally with the increase in reaction time. More or less the same negatively charged C-dots are produced at 5.0 and 6.0min. At 6.0min, more positively charged and neutral C-dots are obtained. Further increase in reaction time (>6.0min) results in similar electropherograms (not shown). In conclusion, the yields and proportions of positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots species are pretty much dependent on reaction time. With longer reaction time, larger amounts of positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots species in a C-dots product could be obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ratio of amine to carboxylic acid groups on C-dots product
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of C-dots follows the initial condensation of CA and EDA to form polymeric amide gel and then carbonized to C-dots. The ratio of amine (in EDA) to carboxylic acid (in CA) groups could affect the PL performance of the as-synthesized C-dots which is closely related to the C-dots surface functionality [10,11]. Thus, we speculate that the ratio of NH2/COOH should determine the yield of C-dots as well as the surface-attached amine and carboxylic acid moieties of an as-synthesized C-dots product. As such, a series of C-dots products were synthesized with different mole ratios of NH2/COOH in the initial reagents. Fig. 5 depicts the electropherograms of C-dots synthesized with different mole ratios of NH2/COOH. Peaks labeled with asterisks are neutral C-dots. Peaks migrated before and after neutral C-dots are positively and negatively charged C-dots, respectively. In the absence of CA, no C-dots are formed (Fig. 5a) because no carbon source is available [10]. By contrast, C-dots are obtained in the presence of EDA and CA. More C-dots are produced with the increase in the mole ratio of NH2/COOH. It is interesting to note that positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots are all obtained at 0.25–0.80 mole ratio of NH2/COOH (Fig. 5b–e). When the mole ratio of NH2/COOH is 1.00–2.00, only positively and neutral C-dots are formed (Fig. 5f–h). In addition, largest amounts of neutral C-dots are found at 1.00–1.33 mole ratio of NH2/COOH (Fig. 5f and g). When the mole ratio of NH2/COOH reaches 2.00, most C-dots are positively charged (Fig. 5h). Our results confirm that the formation of different kinds of C-dots is indeed governed by the mole ratio of NH2/COOH in the reagents. At lower mole ratio (0.25–0.80) of NH2/COOH, the C-dots products contain a variety of amine and carboxylic acid functionalized C-dots. By contrast, at higher mole ratio (1.00–2.00) of NH2/COOH, the C-dots products are dominant by the amine functionalized C-dots. In other words, more EDA will result in an increase in the formation of positively charged C-dots. In summary, our CZE demonstrates that both EDA and CA are essential for the formation of C-dots. When the mole ratio of NH2/COOH is 0.67–0.80, the yields of C-dots are the highest and yield positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots species. When the mole ratio of NH2/COOH is ≥1.00, only positively charged and neutral C-dots are formed. Our work could indeed provide an insight on the composition of an as-synthesized C-dots product derived from microwave pyrolysis of CA and EDA, assisting us to synthesize the required C-dots species.

Since our as-synthesized C-dots possess strong PL properties, CZE coupled with LIF detection was also employed to investigate the C-dots product synthesized with 0.67 mole ratio of NH2/COOH. Fig. S14 displays the electrophoregrams monitored at an absorption wavelength of 250nm and fluorescence monitored at excitation/emission wavelengths of 488/550nm. The combination of UV and LIF detection schemes reveals corroborating as well as additional information. For example, the two electropherograms (Fig. S14Aa and b) display similar peak patterns with the complex C-dots mixture being well-separated into three major parts, i.e., neutral, positively and negatively charged fractions. Strong UV and fluorescence signals are observed for Peaks 1 and 2, indicating that positively charged and neutral C-dots possess strong emission. However, weak emission but strong absorption signals are found for Peak 3, inferring that negatively charged C-dots have lower fluorescence quantum yield. The incorporation of the two detection methods provides a more thorough and effective way to study the composition of a C-dots mixture.

Fig. S14B displays the absorption spectra of Peaks 1–3 (from bottom to top) with typical spectral characteristics of C-dots mixture. All the spectra display a distinctive absorption peak at around 350nm (n → π* transitions of CO bonding), whereas their absorption bands below 250nm show some slight differences. Peak 1 has a shoulder band at 233nm while Peaks 2 and 3 are bathochromically shifted to ca. 244nm, possibly attributing to the widening of the energy gap π → π* transition of the aromatic carbons on the C-core by the surface-attached positive-charged moieties.

Fig. S15A displays the electropherograms of C-dots detected by LIF under different pHs. The corresponding μ ep and μ eof are plotted in Fig. S15B. The electrophoretic behavior of the positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots are the same as in Fig. 2. Similarly, the electrophoretic separation of the C-dots product monitored by LIF at various reaction times (Fig. S16) and mole ratio of NH2/COOH (Fig. S17) were performed. Again, the electrophoretic behaviors of all kinds of C-dots are very similar to that of Figs. 4 and 5, respectively. One major difference between the two electropherograms is that the positively and neutral C-dots give stronger emission signals while the negatively charged C-dots are much weaker. It is anticipated that the brighter positively charged and neutral C-dots species could be isolated from the C-dots sample and will be useful as cellular imaging probes.

In summary, we have demonstrated that as-synthesized C-dots derived from microwave pyrolysis of EDA and CA is complex in nature and their composition can be conveniently revealed by CZE. The C-dots samples indeed comprise a mixture of positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots species. The positively and negatively charged C-dots are dominant with ammonium and carboxylate moieties, respectively. For the neutral charged C-dots, it is possible that they possess equal number of ammonium and carboxylate and/or covered with amido moieties. With the increase in pH, the μ ep of the negatively charged C-dots increases, whereas that of the positively charged C-dots decreases.

In attempts for better understanding the experimental conditions to synthesize C-dots, our proposed CZE methodology has been successfully applied to identify various kinds of C-dots species in as-synthesized C-dots products. To our knowledge, this is the first report on studying the effect of reaction time and mole ratio of NH2/COOH of the reagents on the composition of the C-dots product by CZE. It is found that longer reaction times provoke higher yields of C-dots. In the absence of CA, no C-dots are formed. All C-dots species increase proportionally with the increase in mole ratio of NH2/COOH from 0.25 to 0.80, indicating that EDA and CA are essential for synthesizing C-dots. By controlling the mole ratio of NH2/COOH at 1.00–2.00, only positively charged and neutral C-dots are formed, inferring that these C-dots species are surface-attached with mostly ammonium moieties. In essence, our capillary electrophoretic separation approach provides a means to study the reaction time-associated kinetics of C-dots formation and to identify the functionality-associated charge states of each separated C-dots fractions. Our work can facilitate investigations of the effect of mole ratio of NH2/COOH on the variation of surface-functionality of C-dots, providing an insight in optimizing experimental conditions for acquiring C-dots products of desired C-dots species.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the HKBU Faculty Research Grant (FRG2/10-11/112) and Hundred Talent Programme of Shanxi Province are gratefully acknowledged. Q. Hu acknowledges the receipt of a postgraduate studentship from the University Grants Committee of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region. We would express our sincere thanks to Ms Winnie Y.K. Wu of the Institute of Advanced Materials for taking the TEM image and Ms Silva T. Mo of the Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University for acquiring the MALDI-TOF MS. The TEM used in this work was supported by the Special Equipment Grant from the University Grants Committee of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, China (Grant SEG_HKBU06). This work was presented at the 20th Symposium on Chemistry Postgraduate Research in Hong Kong on 27 April 2013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.chroma.2013.07.035.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic procedure, UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra, quantum yield determination of C-dots, IR spectra of C-dots, CA and EDA, TEM images and MALDI-TOF MS of C-dots, supplementary table summarizes functional groups identified on IR spectra of CA, EDA and C-dots, electrophoretic separation of C-dots under alkaline conditions, electropherograms of C-dots mixture using various capillaries, lengths, applied voltages, temperatures, sample injection pressures and times, repeat CZE runs of C-dots sample, RSDs of peak area and migration time for C-dots species, photographic images of C-dots under daylight and UV, electropherograms of C-dots monitored by PDA and LIF, UV spectra of Peak 1–3, and electropherograms of C-dots products monitored by LIF under different run buffer pHs, reaction times and mole ratios of amino/carboxyl groups. This material can be found in the online version at http://www.sciencedirect.com.
